title: UDev rule based usb devices are not handled correctly after replugging
body:
### Issue Description

I recently migrated all my docker containers to podman. There are 34 containers of different types. I was amazed at how easy and straightforward it was.

But one problem remains. I have 2 containers that access devices that are created via a UDev rule. They are USB sticks that create symlinks using a udev rule. These in turn are mounted in the container.

So far it works under podman. The problem under podman is the bind mount, which mounts the inode of the dissolved device.

i.e. after a reboot or when the USB stick is plugged in again, the device is available under a different /dev/USBX. The UDEV rule ensures that it is available under the same symlink. Only the container still points to the old USB device.

This was not a problem under Docker and worked for years.

What is the recommended way to deal with this problem?

Currently, the container has to be recreated, which is absolute overkill. Currently, I only start my 34 containers after a reboot again. Recreating them all on suspicion would slow down the entire startup process extremely and also present me with new problems.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. create a udev rule which creates a symlink between e.g. /dev/USB0 and /dev/myCustomLink
2. mount /dev/myCustomLink to the container
3. unplug the usb device and plug it again
4. mounted symlink inside the container should point to /dev/USB1, but ist points to /dev/USB0


### Describe the results you received

mounted symlink point to the wrong device

### Describe the results you expected

mounted symlink should point to the correct device

### podman info output

comments.body:
Is your container creating a lot of files on its overlay upper directory?  Otherwise recreating the container is not much more expensive than restarting it.

Once resolved, the original symlink does not affect what the container sees.   You could try `--mount type=bind` with the `no-dereference` option help so can bind mount the symlink itself but in that case, you'd need to bind mount `/dev` since the symlink is then resolved inside the container.

@giuseppe I already "fixed" it by mounting the /dev inside the container. No additional mount or mount type was needed. But this is a security hole in my opinion, because it gives also access to other devices.

the container itself is creating a lot of files, because it is initializing several minutes and is downloading a lot of additional "plugins" based on configs. (https://hub.docker.com/r/openhab/openhab)

the reason why I create this bug ticket is that docker was able to handle this and I guess, your goal is to be as compatible as much as possible.

I ported all of my [31 different containers](https://github.com/HolgerHees/smartserver) from docker to podman and this is the only thing which behaves completely differently. The rest had only minor differences where I was able to find a proper replacement.

With podman inspect the related part looks like

```
"Devices": [
                    {
                         "PathOnHost": "/dev/ttyUSB0",
                         "PathInContainer": "/dev/ttyMyTestDevice",
                         "CgroupPermissions": ""
                    }
               ],
```

with docker inspect it looks like

```
 "Mounts": [
            {
                "Type": "bind",
                "Source": "/dev/ttyMyTestDevice",
                "Destination": "/dev/ttyMyTestDevice",
                "Mode": "",
                "RW": true,
                "Propagation": "rprivate"
            }
        ],
```

inside the docker container it looks like
```
/ # ls -al /dev/
total 0
drwxr-xr-x    5 root     root           380 Sep 28 15:15 .
drwxr-xr-x    1 root     root           134 Sep 28 15:15 ..
crw--w----    1 root     tty       136,   0 Sep 28 15:15 console
lrwxrwxrwx    1 root     root            11 Sep 28 15:15 core -> /proc/kcore
lrwxrwxrwx    1 root     root            13 Sep 28 15:15 fd -> /proc/self/fd
crw-rw-rw-    1 root     root        1,   7 Sep 28 15:15 full
drwxrwxrwt    2 root     root            40 Sep 28 15:15 mqueue
crw-rw-rw-    1 root     root        1,   3 Sep 28 15:15 null
lrwxrwxrwx    1 root     root             8 Sep 28 15:15 ptmx -> pts/ptmx
drwxr-xr-x    2 root     root             0 Sep 28 15:15 pts
crw-rw-rw-    1 root     root        1,   8 Sep 28 15:15 random
drwxrwxrwt    2 root     root            40 Sep 28 15:15 shm
lrwxrwxrwx    1 root     root            15 Sep 28 15:15 stderr -> /proc/self/fd/2
lrwxrwxrwx    1 root     root            15 Sep 28 15:15 stdin -> /proc/self/fd/0
lrwxrwxrwx    1 root     root            15 Sep 28 15:15 stdout -> /proc/self/fd/1
crw-rw-rw-    1 root     root        5,   0 Sep 28 15:15 tty
crw-rw----    1 root     490       188,   0 Sep 28 15:14 ttyMyTestDevice
crw-rw-rw-    1 root     root        1,   9 Sep 28 15:15 urandom
crw-rw-rw-    1 root     root        1,   5 Sep 28 15:15 zero
```

on my host it looks like

```
lrwxrwxrwx 1 root  root         12 28. Sep 17:14 /dev/ttyMyTestDevice -> /dev/ttyUSB0
```
I read somewhere since kernel v5.12+ bind mount supports symlinks with the flag AT_SYMLINK_NOFOLLOW on files.
@giuseppe is there a realistic chance that this problem will be fixed sometimes? Or is there another reason why this symlink is always resolved?

Wouldn't like a `HealthCmd` check work ?

I am using this with a DIY `ustreamer` Container and something like this would work and restart the Container whenever the USB Device is unplugged and replugged later.

Relevant Part of the Quadlet Configuration::

```
...

# Devices Configuration
AddDevice=/dev/ms2130-pc01-video0:/dev/ms2130-pc01-video0

# This should work
HealthCmd=v4l2-ctl -d /dev/ms2130-pc01-video0 --all || exit 1
HealthInterval=15s
HealthRetries=1
HealthOnFailure=restart
```

Note that you need `HealthOnFailure=restart` otherwise nothing would happen.

Furthermore at least in my Case `HealthCmd=stat /dev/ms2130-pc01-video0 || exit 1` would NOT work since the Device always exists but goes from Mode `0666` (`crw-rw----`) to 000 (`c---------`).

this will not work. Because podman will always mount the symbolic link target inode id. This mean, if you unplug the device and replug, it will have a new inode id, but the container will still try to mount the old inode id. Also after a restart.

I don't understand why this simple, but important behavior will not be implemented. With docker, you see that this is possible. My workaround is currently to mount the whole dev folder.  In this case the symbolic link target is always available inside the container. But I don't like this way, because this makes all devices available inside the container.

In my scenario I migrated ~30 docker container to podman without any further problems. Except this one.

I don't understand your use Case because to be honest it 100% works for me.

I require a Reboot of `ustreamer` Container whenever the Device is disconnected and then reconnected (even if, on the host, it maps to the same `/dev/video4` Device).

Note that the `HealthCmd` MUST **NOT** only rely on something simple like `ls -l` or `stat` to check the mere Existance of the `/dev/ms2130-pc01-video0` Device.

Because, when disconnected, that will show up as `000` to the Output of `stat` (`c---------`).

So you could for instance check with
```
stat -c %a /dev/ms2130-pc01-video0
```

If that is Zero (`0`) then it's Faulty, otherwise (in my Case if it's `0666`, `0660` isn't sufficient for Permissions apparently) then it's Fine.

```
if [ $(stat -c %a /dev/ms2130-pc01-video0) -eq 0 ]; then false; else true; fi
```

EDIT 1: @HolgerHees, how does it look inside the Container (Output of `stat /dev/ttyMyTestDevice` BEFORE Disconnecting and AFTER Disconnecting) ?

When connected:
```
/ustreamer # stat /dev/ms2130-pc01-video0 
  File: /dev/ms2130-pc01-video0
  Size: 0         	Blocks: 0          IO Block: 4096   character special file
Device: 6h/6d	Inode: 1841        Links: 1     Device type: 51,4
Access: (0666/crw-rw-rw-)  Uid: (65534/  nobody)   Gid: (65534/  nobody)
Access: 2025-06-02 06:36:46.079289503 +0000
Modify: 2025-06-02 06:36:46.079289503 +0000
Change: 2025-06-02 06:36:46.079289503 +0000
```

When disconnected:
```
/ustreamer # stat /dev/ms2130-pc01-video0 
  File: /dev/ms2130-pc01-video0
  Size: 0         	Blocks: 0          IO Block: 4096   character special file
Device: 6h/6d	Inode: 1841        Links: 0     Device type: 51,4
Access: (0000/c---------)  Uid: (65534/  nobody)   Gid: (65534/  nobody)
Access: 2025-06-02 06:36:46.079289503 +0000
Modify: 2025-06-02 06:36:46.079289503 +0000
Change: 2025-06-02 08:52:33.679297398 +0000
```

When reconnected (**without** Reboot):
```
/ustreamer # stat /dev/ms2130-pc01-video0 
  File: /dev/ms2130-pc01-video0
  Size: 0         	Blocks: 0          IO Block: 4096   character special file
Device: 6h/6d	Inode: 1841        Links: 0     Device type: 51,4
Access: (0000/c---------)  Uid: (65534/  nobody)   Gid: (65534/  nobody)
Access: 2025-06-02 06:36:46.079289503 +0000
Modify: 2025-06-02 06:36:46.079289503 +0000
Change: 2025-06-02 08:52:33.679297398 +0000
```

Not sure about your vs my User/Group Mapping. Mine maps to `nobody:nobody` for some Reason.

I was running with the Following Security Options in the Quadlet, for Reference (many Things to Troubleshoot, I gave it a bit Lax Permissions for now):
```
NoNewPrivileges=true

SecurityLabelDisable=true

CgroupsMode=no-conmon

AddCapability=CAP_PERFMON
AddCapability=SYS_ADMIN
AddCapability=CAP_SYS_ADMIN
AddCapability=PERFMON

GroupAdd=keep-groups
```

EDIT 2: @HolgerHees how does `udev` Rule look like ? Mine seems to map consistently to `/dev/video4` although I didn't plug 10 different Adapters to try to force the Charge.

I base my UDEV Rule on the Serial Number, Vendor ID and Device ID.

EDIT 3: I just tried with a Second Adapter, trying to force the `/dev/ms2130-pc01-video0` to change Mapping. Started at `/dev/video7`, unplugged, replugged, became `/dev/video4`, Container automatically rebooted. Unplugged again, replugged once more, became `/dev/video5`, Container automatically rebooted once again.

And after Reboot, everything was working as it was supposed to 😉.

So for all Intents and Purposes it seems to be working for me on Fedora 42 aarch64 (RockPro64 SBC).

You have a quite old Version (4.9.5) so it might be caused by that ... I remember plenty of "weird Stuff" back then at least.

If you talk about Quadlet, I guess your container is not stopped and started again. I guess Quadlet is destroying and recreating the container and this works for me too. But I want to restart the container. This means stop and start and not destroy and recreate.

I create custom system services files which interacts with podman via "podman start" and "podman stop"

~I think it's destroyed and being recreated.~


The Question is: why would that be a Problem for you ?

EDIT 1: I think it's actually just getting restarted ...

That also matches what I set for `HealthOnFailure=restart`.

The problem for me is that I depend on >30 containers. Some of them need several minutes for the initial start. 

e.g. openhab is fetching addons initially

and most other containers are much faster in just restarting instead of recreating. For me it is not acceptable that it takes so long. But I don't want to start a discussion about this topic. I can't influence some of the container and have to accept how they work.

and stopping a container and restarting is a offered behavior for docker and podman. And docker handles this perfectly. So why not podman. Podman want to be the successor for docker.

...

I just read the documentation about quadlet and it always recreate the container. Also if you specify "HealthOnFailure=restart" it recreates the container at the end. 

This means if you use quadlet, you are not affected by this scenario.

That's not what my Log above shows though (`restart`, `init`, `start`).

But it's true that they do NOT reccomend using `restart` if there is also a `restart` in the Unit File (which mine has):
https://docs.podman.io/en/v5.5.0/markdown/podman-run.1.html#health-on-failure-action

But yeah, if that doesn't help you, then I don't have anything else I can add 😅.

the first line in your log file shows that a fresh image is pulled and the second line in your log file shows that a new container is created.

but anyway. Thanks for discussing.

Ah yeah, I missed the 3 Lines at the very Top, you are right.

There might be an Option hidden somewhere still but since I never needed to avoid destroy/create I never really looked into it.

You are Welcome 😃.
